data.title: ‘Volatility is the name of the game’ from now on – MetLife’s chief market strategist
data.description:
The U.S. is still getting a recession, according to Drew Matus, MetLife Investment Management chief market strategist.

During a CNBC interview, Matus said he continues to anticipate a recession and stated that volatility is “the name of the game going forward.”

He pointed to the impact of equity market volatility (VIX) on upper-income consumers, who have been a key driver of spending. This volatility can create a “hot stove mentality” or wealth effect, leading these consumers to potentially pull back on consumption.

Companies also face significant uncertainty due to tariff rates that could change depending on manufacturing location. “This makes it difficult for firms to plan investments and manage costs,” potentially leading them to consider how to save money, which can translate to staffing decisions, he said.

Survey data suggests upper-income consumers are increasingly concerned about job security and expect lower real wage gains. This prompts them to “start saving for a rainy day,” leading to reduced spending on non-essentials like dining out or vacations, Matus added.

He believes these behavioral shifts, if widespread enough, can become “a self-fulfilling prophecy toward slower economic activity.”

He noted that survey data indicates consumers are becoming increasingly fearful for the outlook at an unusual speed.

While a formal recession may or may not occur, Matus concluded that whatever economic conditions the market is heading into are unlikely to “feel all that great.” Consumer fear is a key factor, setting the stage for a significant slowing in growth.

data.title: Humanoid robot wars: Tesla Optimus can dance and work a factory shift as rivals close in
data.description:
Tesla's (NASDAQ:TSLA) Optimus humanoid robot has been attracting a lot of attention this week. The company has continued to showcase the notable advancements with the Optimus humanoid robot, as recent demonstrations have shown the prototype performing complex dance and ballet moves, highlighting significant improvements in mobility and dexterity. 

Notably, the viral Optimus movements were learned entirely in simulation and transferred to the real robot without additional training, leveraging Tesla's (NASDAQ:TSLA) reinforcement learning expertise. Enthusiasts have noted that the robot now walks with a much more humanlike gait, upright posture, and natural arm swing, marking a leap from earlier, more awkward prototypes

While Optimus appears to be closer to being in a production-ready state, Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk said the dancing Optimus was still very far from its final form. Previously, Musk stated that the company aims to produce about 5,000 units in 2025, with enough parts to potentially build up to 12,000 robots if the ramp-up goes smoothly. The robots are currently being deployed primarily within Tesla's (TSLA) own factories, especially for tasks on the upcoming Cybercab production line at the Texas Gigafactory.

Looking further ahead, Elon Musk has suggested that Optimus could be sold to external customers as soon as 2026, with a target price between $25,000 and $30,000 per unit.

Some of the other companies working on humanoid robots include Figure AI (backed by Microsoft (MSFT) and OpenAI), Boston Dynamics, Agility Robotics, Apptronik, X1, Unitree, UBTECH Robotics, Hanson Robotics, Sanctuary AI (partnered with Microsoft (MSFT) and Magna International (MGA)), and Xpeng (XPEV). Apple (AAPL) is also reportedly actively exploring both humanoid and non-humanoid robots, although its work is still in the early proof-of-concept stage. Meanwhile, Meta Platforms (META) has launched a new division within its Reality Labs unit dedicated to developing AI-powered humanoid robots.

China is rapidly emerging as a global leader in the development and deployment of humanoid robots, with several companies at the forefront of the technological wave and apeparing to be ahead of Tesla (TSLA). Shenzhen-based UBTECH is one of China's most well-known robotics firms, specializing in AI-powered humanoid robots. Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) is also a rising player.

data.title: SA analyst downgrades: AMD, PLUG, WBD, BITF
data.description:
Here are the latest rating downgrades by Seeking Alpha analysts:

Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD). Hold to sell by Bill Maurer. "With AMD shares rallying more than 60% from their recent lows, I think this could be a good opportunity to sell into strength. The valuation is basically matching that of Nvidia (NVDA) for this year, despite AMD providing a weaker growth picture than its main Data Center competitor."

Plug Power (NASDAQ:PLUG). Hold to sell by Henrik Alex. "The proposed expiration of the hydrogen production tax credit by the end of this year presents an existential threat to the company. With the company's business plan now in jeopardy and limited funding options beyond this year, I am downgrading PLUG shares from Hold to Sell."

Warner Bros. Discovery. (NASDAQ:WBD). Buy to hold by Howard Jay Klein. 

Bitfarms (NASDAQ:BITF). Buy to hold by Mandela Amoussou.

data.title: House panel gathers new allegations on Pfizer’s COVID vaccine success: WSJ
data.description:
New allegations against Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) have surfaced as part of an investigation launched by the House Judiciary Committee over claims that the pharma giant deliberately delayed announcing the success of its COVID vaccine developed with BioNTech (BNTX), The Wall Street Journal reported Thursday.

The probe centers on claims allegedly made by former Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) scientist Phil Dormitzer, who has told his colleagues at his new employer, GSK (GSK), that the U.S. company waited to share results of its COVID vaccine until after the U.S. presidential election in 2020.

Citing a letter from the House panel, the Journal reported that Dormitzer, once Pfizer’s (NYSE:PFE) chief scientific officer responsible for viral vaccines, has said he was part of a deliberate attempt to “slow down” the vaccine’s testing.

According to the letter, GSK staffers recalled that Dormitzer “was clear that this was not a situation of delaying disclosure of completed results, but was a situation of slowing down results before disclosure became necessary.”

Dormitzer has told his colleagues there that three of the most senior staffers at Pfizer (PFE) R&D were “involved in a decision to deliberately slow down clinical testing so that it would not be complete prior to the results of the presidential election that year,” according to GSK’s (GSK) report to the house panel.

The panel on Thursday requested Pfizer (PFE) to submit documents related to testing, and also sent a letter to Dormitzer asking for similar material from him and an interview by May 29.

Pfizer (PFE) spokesperson Amy Rose confirmed the receipt of the letter and said the company “would respond directly to the committee.” The company’s COVID vaccine development was “driven by science and guided by the U.S. FDA back in 2020,” Rose noted, adding, “Theories to the contrary are simply untrue and being manufactured.”

data.title: National Grid backs five-year investment plan as annual profit beats forecast
data.description:
National Grid (NYSE:NGG) +3.1% in Thursday's trading after saying it continues to expect a compounded 6%-8% annual growth rate in underlying earnings per share from a 2025 baseline of 73.3 pence.

The British energy infrastructure company said a strong performance across the business, reflected in better than expected underlying annual profit, underpins its plans to invest £60B (~$79B) in energy networks over the next five years.

Pretax profit rose 20% to £3.65B in the year to March 31 from £3.05B in the previous year, full-year earnings jumped 8% to 60 pence/share from 55.5 pence/share, or by 1.7% on an underlying basis to 73.3p from 72.1p, ahead of 72.3p consensus, but revenue fell 7.4% Y/Y to £18.38B from £19.85B in the previous year.

Full-year underlying operating profit increased 12% to £5.36B from £4.77B a year ago, ahead of a company compiled consensus of £5.29B.

National Grid's (NYSE:NGG) reiterated five-year earnings guidance means there could be some upgrades to consensus expectations, J.P. Morgan analysts Pavan Mahbubani and Mukund Verma said.

data.title: Seth Klarman's Baupost adds FIS, SGI stakes, dumps SNRE, HUM, among Q1 moves
data.description:
Baupost Group, the hedge fund led by billionaire investor Seth Klarman, took fresh stakes in Fidelity National Information Services (FIS), with 3.49M shares, and Somnigroup International (SGI), with 703.4K shares, during Q1 2025, according to a 13F filing published Thursday.

The firm boosted its position sizes in Alphabet Class C stock (GOOG), to 2.08M shares in Q1 from 1.43M shares in Q4 2024, Restaurant Brands International (QSR), to 3.89M from 2.90M, and Eagle Materials (EXP), to 909.6K from 638.2K.

Meanwhile, it exited its holdings in Humana (HUM) and Sunrise Communications AG (SNRE).

Reduced positions include Willis Towers Watson (WTW), to 1.53M from 1.84M, and Clarivate (CLVT), to 10.8M from 36.3M, the filing showed.

data.title: ETF inflows cut through the noise of market uncertainty as a vote of investor confidence
data.description:
Despite a volatile year marked by sharp rallies, steep declines, and persistent macroeconomic uncertainty—from trade tensions, inflationary pressures, interest rate sentiment, and recession fears—the underlying strength of the market has remained resilient. This resilience is most clearly reflected in exchange traded fund flows.

As 2025 progresses, investors are on pace to pour in over $1T into ETFs for a second consecutive year, with nearly half of that capital flowing into U.S. equities, signaling continued confidence in the American market despite the noise that is being echoed. 

So far this year the ETF sphere has absorbed $400B in net new money which equates to a $4.4B a day influx led by the world’s largest ETF, the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:VOO) which has taken in just over $60B this year. VOO’s massive intake of capital has the fund on pace to garner $164B by years end. 

Moreover, see below Wall Street’s top 10 ETF inflow leaders for the 2025 trading year: 

No. 10: SPDR Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLD), +$5.76B.

No. 9: JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:JEPQ), +$6.32B.

No. 8: Vanguard Growth Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VUG), +$6.49B.

No. 7: Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF (NASDAQ:QQQM), +$7.44B.

No. 6: iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (NASDAQ:IBIT), +$7.76B.

No. 5: SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (NYSEARCA:BIL), +$9.44B. 

No. 4: SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPLG), +$13.20B. 

No. 3: Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:VTI), +$14.54B. 

No. 2: iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:SGOV), +$15.86B. 

No. 1: Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:VOO), +$60.47B.

ETF data flow is per Bloomberg.

data.title: Fiserv stock slumps after CFO comments on Q2 Clover volume growth
data.description:
Fiserv (NYSE:FI) stock slid 12% in late morning trading on Thursday after the company's chief financial officer said he expects volume growth of its Clover retail payment system in Q2 will be similar to Q1.

"We expect second quarter to be generally similar to first quarter, in terms of the reported growth rate from a volume standpoint," Fiserv CFO Bob Hau at an industry conference.

Thursday's stock decline comes less than a month after Fiserv (NYSE:FI) stock lost almost 19% after it released its Q1 2025 earnings. "We were certainly disappointed with the reaction — unexpected," he said.

"What we have learned through this is that there is an intense focus on Clover volume," Hau noted. Clover's volume growth of 8% Y/Y in Q1 vs.14% Y/Y in Q4. Part of that growth deceleration came from a gateway conversion that brought more merchants in Clover's system starting in Q1 2024. That represented about 2 percentage point headwind. Other factors included an extra business day in 2024's Q1 due to leap year, slowing in Canada growth, and the timing of Easter.

Hau sees the gateway headwind "increasing a bit" in Q2. "We've been converting that gateway for about a year."  Q2 is when "you have the full quarter impact of what was going on in Q1." He expects that to subside in subsequent quarters.

Volume growth is one element of Clover's growth, he said. Hardware and value-added services are also key drivers of revenue growth. "We saw good hardware sales in the second quarter, which actually followed very good hardware sales in the fourth quarter, and we saw very good value-added services penetration," he said. That measure went from 22% to 24% in the first quarter of this year.

He emphasized that Clover's Q1 sales rose 27% Y/Y vs. 24% in Q4.

We remain confident in delivering $3.5B of revenue by the end of the year, he added.
